steps.title: Locate the finite verb
steps.content: The relative clause begins with the subject 'whose work' and later contains the finite verb 'encourages': 'whose work ______ the richness of Betham's poems and the poet's historical importance to the Romantic literary movement encourages a broader rethinking of British Romanticism itself.' Because 'encourages' already serves as the main verb of that clause, the blank cannot add another finite verb for the same subject.

steps.title: Reject extra finite verbs
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'has highlighted': This is a finite present-perfect verb. Pairing it with 'encourages' would create two finite verbs for 'whose work' without a coordinating conjunction.
  • Choice B - 'is highlighting': This is also finite. It would compete with 'encourages' for the role of main verb in the same relative clause.
  • Choice D - 'highlights': This finite verb agrees with 'work,' but it still creates the same uncoordinated two-verb structure: 'whose work highlights the richness and importance encourages a broader rethinking.'

steps.title: Use the participial modifier
steps.content: • Choice C - 'highlighting': This is a non-finite present participle, so it can modify 'work' without replacing or duplicating the main verb. The sentence then reads as a grammatical clause in which Bailey's work, highlighting Betham's richness and importance, encourages a broader rethinking. The answer is C.
